<!-- Topic 1: Review of Menu-Driven Programs -->
<!-- Total slides: 4 -->

# Menu-Driven Programs

---

## What keeps a menu program running between choices? {.smaller}

Menu-driven programs combine a loop with a decision structure — the loop keeps the menu alive, the decision routes each choice. This is a pattern worth naming.

::: notes
Total slides: 4
:::

<!-- Slide 1 -->

---

## Menu-Driven Program Structure

A menu program has three parts working together:

- **The loop** — keeps the program running until the user chooses to quit
- **The menu display** — shows options each iteration
- **The dispatcher** — an `if/else` or `switch` that acts on the choice

The do-while is a natural fit: display first, then check whether to continue.

<!-- Slide 2 -->

---

## Light Input Validation on the Choice

```{.cpp}
int choice;
do {
    displayMenu();
    cin >> choice;

    if (choice < 1 || choice > 4) {
        cout << "Invalid choice. Enter 1-4.\n";
        continue;
    }

    dispatch(choice);

} while (choice != 4);
```

Validate the choice before dispatching. `continue` skips the dispatch and re-displays the menu.

<!-- Slide 3 -->

---

## Summary

A menu-driven program is a do-while loop wrapped around a dispatcher — the loop provides persistence, the condition provides exit. Light input validation catches bad choices before they reach the dispatcher.

<!-- Slide 4 -->
